subject: Nephrology
title: Renal cell carcinoma
preambleMarkdown:
# Renal cell carcinoma

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Renal cell carcinoma is a neoplastic, premalignant, or tumor-related topic in nephrology.
- This article focuses on kidney function, electrolytes, acid-base balance, urinalysis, renal replacement therapy, and drug adjustment.
- The practical clinical priority is malignant potential, staging, treatment intent, and complications.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Renal cell carcinoma to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Renal cell carcinoma is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is malignant potential, staging, treatment intent, and complications.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Benign, premalignant, in situ, localized invasive, regional, metastatic, recurrent, or treatment-related state.
- Histology, grade, stage, biomarkers, and performance status.
- Curative, disease-control, surveillance, symptom-relief, or end-of-life treatment intent.
- Screen-detected, symptomatic, incidental, familial, or therapy-associated presentation.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Renal cell carcinoma depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In nephrology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Renal cell carcinoma.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Renal cell carcinoma disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Renal cell carcinoma should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Renal cell carcinoma first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Renal cell carcinoma, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Mass effect, bleeding, obstruction, pain, weight loss, constitutional symptoms, paraneoplastic findings, or incidental screening abnormality may occur.
- Red flags depend on anatomic site and include neurologic compromise, airway compromise, uncontrolled bleeding, or sepsis.
- Treatment history matters because recurrence and treatment toxicity can mimic new disease.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Renal cell carcinoma, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused nephrology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Renal cell carcinoma presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Renal cell carcinoma with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Renal cell carcinoma is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Renal cell carcinoma should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis with tissue when required, stage before definitive therapy when possible, and define treatment intent.
- Use multidisciplinary planning for surgery, radiation, systemic therapy, surveillance, fertility, rehabilitation, and palliation.
- Treat oncologic emergencies immediately while definitive cancer planning continues.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Renal cell carcinoma is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses malignant potential, staging, treatment intent, and complications.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical referral is needed for resectable disease, biopsy planning, obstruction, bleeding, perforation, cord compression, airway compromise, or palliation.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Renal cell carcinoma, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Renal cell carcinoma may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Renal cell carcinoma patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Renal cell carcinoma, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Renal cell carcinoma in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Renal cell carcinoma, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Renal cell carcinoma does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Renal cell carcinoma may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Renal cell carcinoma, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Renal cell carcinoma as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis and staging are separate steps.
- Treatment depends on stage, histology, biomarkers, performance status, comorbidity, and patient goals.
- Know emergency presentations: cord compression, tumor lysis, febrile neutropenia, airway compromise, and superior vena cava syndrome.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Renal cell carcinoma draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

Source: Core clinical source notes. Imported on 2026-07-14.

### Source Excerpt: Renal Cell Carcinoma

_Source lines: 26588-26902._

```text
Renal Cell Carcinoma
A. General Characteristics
1. It is twice as common in men as in women.

2. RCC comprises about 85% of primary renal cancers (transitional
cell of the renal pelvis is the second most common).

3. Most cases are sporadic; less than 2% occur as part of autosomal
dominant von Hippel-Lindau syndrome. The pathogenesis is
unknown.

4. Usually discovered when localized to kidney or with regional
lymph node spread
5. Areas of metastasis include the lung, liver, brain, and bone. Tumor
thrombus can invade the renal vein or inferior vena cava, resulting
in hematogenous dissemination.

Quick HIT
Up to 20% to 30% of cases of RCC are discovered incidentally on a
CT scan or ultrasound performed for other reasons.

B. Risk Factors
1. Cigarette smoking
2. Analgesics (high use), particular phenacetin-containing medications
but acetaminophen, NSAIDs, and aspirin have also been implicated
3. Adult polycystic kidney disease
4. Chronic dialysis (multicystic disease develops)
5. Exposure to heavy metals (mercury, cadmium)

## 6. Hypertension

## 7. Obesity

C. Clinical Features
1. Hematuria is the most common symptom (gross or microscopic)-
occurs in 70% of the patients.

2. Abdominal or flank pain-occurs in 50% of the patients.

3. Abdominal (flank) mass-occurs in 40% of the patients.

4. Findings seen in many neoplasms: Weight loss, fever, anemia of
chronic inflammation.

5. Paraneoplastic syndromes (uncommon)-these tumors can
ectopically secrete erythropoietin (causing polycythemia), PTH-like
hormone (causing hypercalcemia), renin (causing HTN), cortisol
(causing Cushing syndrome), or gonadotropins (causing
feminization or masculinization).

Quick HIT
The classic triad of hematuria, flank pain, and abdominal mass
occurs in less than 10% of patients with RCC.

D. Diagnosis
1. Abdominal CT (with and without contrast)-optimal test for
diagnosis and staging; perform if ultrasound shows a mass or cysts.

Cannot distinguish benign and malignant solid masses, both are
usually empirically resected.

2. Renal ultrasound-occasionally used for detection of renal mass
and to distinguish from cyst.

E. Treatment
1. Partial nephrectomy (open or laparoscopic) can be used for smaller
tumors, particularly when bilateral or with associated genetic
syndrome, to save renal parenchyma and function.

2. Radical nephrectomy (excision of kidney and adrenal gland,
including Gerota fascia with excision of nodal tissue along the renal
hilum) when any invasion into surrounding vasculature or adipose

is noted.

3. For advanced disease, debulking surgery and adjuvant
chemotherapy is often used.

Quick HIT
Transitional cell carcinoma accounts for 90% of all bladder cancers.

Other types are squamous cell (5%) and adenocarcinoma (2%).

Bladder Cancer
A. General Characteristics
1. Bladder carcinoma is the most common type of tumor of the
genitourinary tract; 90% of bladder cancers are transitional cell
carcinomas. Transitional cell carcinomas can occur anywhere from
the kidney to the bladder (e.g., renal pelvis, ureter), but 90% of
these carcinomas are in the bladder.

2. The most common route of spread is direct local extension to
surrounding tissues (rather than hematologic/lymphatic
dissemination).

3. It is likely to recur after removal.

4. Risk factors
a. Cigarette smoking (the major identified risk factor)
b. Industrial carcinogens (aniline dye, azo dyes)
c. Long-term treatment with cyclophosphamide (may cause
hemorrhagic cystitis and increase the risk of transitional cell
carcinoma)
B. Clinical Features
1. Initial presenting sign is hematuria in most cases (painless
hematuria is the classic presentation). It is more likely to be gross,
but can be microscopic as well.

2. Irritable bladder symptoms, such as dysuria, frequency, and/or
urgency.

3. Pain or obstructive symptoms may be seen when carcinoma
becomes more invasive.

4. Physical examination is usually unremarkable.

C. Diagnosis
1. Urinalysis and urine culture-to detect RBCs and confirm
hematuria as red urine may also be due to various foods or
medications (e.g., beets, dyes, phenazopyridine, myoglobin,

Serratia) and to rule out infection
2. Urine cytology-to detect malignant cells
3. Cystoscopy and biopsy-definitive test which should be performed
in all patients >40 years old or with risk factors for bladder cancer
4. Chest radiograph and CT scan-for staging
5. IVP is superior at detecting certain small tumors of ureter or renal
pelvis, it can also be used if CT unavailable
A 57-year-old man presents to his physician for his annual examination.

He has a history of hypertension, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
(COPD), and benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). On examination, there
is hyper-resonance to percussion of both lung fields and a diffusely
enlarged, nontender prostate on rectal examination. His blood work is
unremarkable, but urine studies show 12 RBCs per high-power field.

There are no dysmorphic RBCs or RBC casts, and there are no other cells
or protein. He denies any fevers, flank or groin pain, episodes of gross
hematuria, or dysuria. A repeat urinalysis 1 week later confirms the
presence of microscopic hematuria, and his urine culture is negative. He
elects to undergo further workup with a CT scan of the abdomen and
pelvis with and without contrast, which is unremarkable.

What is the next step in management?

A. Measure PSA
B. Transrectal ultrasound and biopsy of the prostate
C. Intravenous pyelogram
D. Renal biopsy
E. Cystoscopy
F. Reassurance
- The answer is E: Cystoscopy. Microscopic hematuria is defined as
>=3 RBCs per high-power field on urine sediment microscopy, and
should be confirmed with a repeat study given the high incidence of
transient hematuria (which is usually benign but in older patients is
still associated with an increased risk of malignancy). (D)
Glomerulonephritis is a potential cause of hematuria and may lead to
a renal biopsy if suspected, but in this case it is unlikely given that
there were no dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, or other suggestive
findings. In the absence of infection or glomerular disease, urine
cytology (to screen for infections and neoplasms) and a CT scan (to
screen for nephrolithiasis, renal neoplasms, etc.) should be
performed. (C) An IV pyelogram may be used to visualize the urinary
tract, but a CT scan is a better imaging modality and was already
performed in this case. The next step is therefore cystoscopy, which
will help to visualize the bladder for cancer. Remember that in
microscopic hematuria, in the absence of an identifiable cause
(infection or glomerular disease), all patients should receive

radiologic imaging of the urinary tract and cystoscopy. (A, B) This
patient has a history of BPH and consistent findings on examination
(diffusely enlarged prostate), making prostate cancer a less likely
explanation for this patient's hematuria. PSA screening may be
discussed with the patient in the future, but a cystoscopy needs to be
performed next. (F) In the presence of BPH, new fragile blood vessels
form and may rupture causing hematuria; however, more serious
causes of hematuria still need to be ruled out.

D. Treatment (depends on the stage of the disease)
1. For surgical candidates, transurethral resection of bladder tumor
(TURBT) is used for noninvasive disease, whereas chemotherapy
with cystectomy is used for muscle-invasive disease. There is also a
role for radiation therapy and systemic chemotherapy for certain
patients.

Testicular Cancer
A. General Characteristics
1. Most common in men 20 to 35 years of age, but can occur in men
of any age
2. Has a relatively high cure rate compared with other cancers

## 3. Types

a. Germ cell tumors (account for 95% of all testicular cancers)-
most common in men 20 to 40 years of age; curable in >95%
of the cases
- Seminomas (35%)-most common; slow growth and late
invasion; most radiosensitive
- Nonseminomatous (65%)-usually contain cells from at least
two of the following four types (mixed cell type): embryonal
carcinoma (high malignant potential, hemorrhage and
necrosis are common; metastases to the abdominal
lymphatics and the lungs may occur as an early event);
choriocarcinoma (most aggressive type; rare; metastases
usually occur by time of diagnosis); teratoma (rarely
metastasize); yolk sac carcinoma (rare in men, usually
occurs in young boys)
d. Nongerm cell tumors (account for 5% of all testicular cancers)-
are usually benign.

- Leydig cell tumors are hormonally active-most are benign
and are treated with surgery. Prognosis is poor if metastasis
occurs. They may secrete a variety of steroid hormones,
including estrogen and androgens, and are associated with

precocious puberty in children and gynecomastia in adults
- Sertoli cell tumors are usually benign
Quick HIT
In a patient with a scrotal mass, perform a careful physical
examination to determine its site of origin because testicular
cancers are almost always malignant, whereas extratesticular
tumors within the scrotum are almost always benign.

B. Risk Factors
1. Cryptorchidism-surgical correction reduces but does not eliminate
risk
2. Klinefelter syndrome
C. Clinical Features
1. Painless mass/lump/firmness of the testicle-because of lack of
pain, may go unnoticed by the patient until advanced.

2. While pain is rare, a "dull ache" in the pelvis is often described.

3. Gynecomastia may be present because some of the
nonseminomatous germ cell tumors produce gonadotropins.

D. Diagnosis
1. Physical examination (testicular mass)
2. Testicular ultrasound-initial test for localizing the tumor
3. Tumor markers-helpful in diagnosis, staging, and monitoring
response to therapy
a. beta-hCG
- Always elevated in choriocarcinoma
- May be elevated in other types of nonseminomatous germ
cell tumors as well
d. AFP
- Increased in embryonal tumors (in 80% of the cases)
- Choriocarcinoma and seminoma never have an elevated AFP
4. CT scan and chest radiograph for staging
5. Biopsy is NOT performed due to concern for seeding/metastasizing
the tumor
Quick HIT
Differential Diagnosis of Testicular Cancer
- Varicocele-dilated, tortuous veins in testicle

- Testicular torsion
- Spermatocele (testicular cyst)
- Hydrocele (fluid in testicle)
- Epididymitis
- Lymphoma
E. Treatment
1. If testicular cancer is suspected based on physical examination or
ultrasound, the testicle should be removed surgically (to confirm
diagnosis). An inguinal approach is used because a scrotal incision
may lead to tumor seeding of the scrotum.

2. After orchiectomy, perform a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and
pelvis for staging.

3. Perform beta-hCG and AFP measurement after orchiectomy for
comparison with the preoperative values.

4. Further treatment depends on the histology of the tumor.

a. Seminoma-inguinal orchiectomy and radiation (very
radiosensitive).

b. Nonseminomatous disease-orchiectomy and retroperitoneal
lymph node dissection with or without chemotherapy.

Quick HIT
A young man with a firm, painless testicular mass should be
presumed to have testicular cancer until proven otherwise.

Penile Cancer
- The peak incidence of this tumor is in men in their seventh decade.

- Circumcision may have a protective effect because penile cancer is
very rare in those who have been circumcised.

- It is associated with herpes simplex virus and HPV 18 infection.

- It presents as an exophytic mass on the penis.

- Treatment of the primary disease is local excision.

Quick HIT
Do not confuse testicular cancer with testicular torsion or
epididymitis.

Miscellaneous Conditions
```
